draft.introduction: Matthew 23:1–39 contains Jesus’s extended denunciation of the scribes and Pharisees, the most sustained polemic in the Synoptic Gospels. From Codex Sinaiticus, fourth century.

translation.text: Then Jesus said to the crowds and to his disciples, “The scribes and the Pharisees sit on Moses’s seat, so do and observe whatever they tell you, but not the works they do. For they preach, but do not practice.”
translation.original_text: τοτε ο ιησους ελαλησεν τοις οχλοις και τοις μαθηταις αυτου

translation.text: O Jerusalem, Jerusalem, the city that kills the prophets and stones those who are sent to it! How often would I have gathered your children together as a hen gathers her brood under her wings, and you were not willing! Behold, your house is left to you desolate.

translator_notes.note: The maternal image of the hen gathering her brood is striking in a passage otherwise marked by severity. It attributes to Jesus a feminine metaphor for divine care drawn from the Hebrew Bible (Deuteronomy 32:11, Ruth 2:12).
